source: iConnections Global Alts 2025 — The Future of AI
summary: We are in year 3 of a decade-long AI infrastructure supercycle analogous to the 1990s PC/client-server boom
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI infrastructure spending is in year three of a decade-long supercycle directly analogous to the 1990s PC and client-server buildout, and that it is far too early for investors to turn bearish on the theme. He supports this view by citing the extraordinary compounding of infrastructure-era winners — Oracle, Sun Microsystems, Cisco, EMC, Microsoft, and Dell — which collectively grew at roughly 40% per year for a full decade during that cycle. His structural claim is that the current AI buildout will follow a similar multi-year trajectory, meaning the primary beneficiaries are still in the early innings of a long compounding period. The investment implication is to maintain or increase exposure to AI infrastructure and platform companies rather than fading the theme on near-term concerns. No specific price targets or near-term catalysts are cited; the thesis is explicitly a long-duration, high-conviction structural view.
detail: Baker draws explicit parallel to Oracle, Sun Microsystems, Cisco, EMC, Microsoft, Dell compounding at 40% per year for a decade during the PC/client-server buildout. He frames current AI as entering year 3 of an equivalent cycle, arguing it is far too early to get bearish on AI/technology.
confidence_evidence: Baker uses a specific historical analog with precise data (40% CAGR for a decade), frames it as a structural multi-year view, and explicitly warns against premature bearishness.
quote_fragment: year three of AI... compounded at 40% per year for a decade

ticker_audit.reasoning: NVDA is the dominant supplier of AI accelerators and is the clearest analog to Cisco in the 1990s infrastructure buildout Baker describes — a picks-and-shovels infrastructure winner in the current AI supercycle. Qualifies as a direct beneficiary of this structural thesis.

MSFT is a major AI platform and infrastructure layer (Azure, OpenAI partnership) and is a reasonable analog to the Microsoft of the 1990s PC cycle Baker explicitly names. Qualifies as a direct beneficiary.

AVGO is a major AI networking and custom silicon supplier, making it a direct infrastructure beneficiary of the AI supercycle thesis Baker describes. Analogous to the networking/infrastructure winners of the 1990s cycle.

While GOOGL is involved in AI, Baker's thesis is specifically about the AI infrastructure supercycle and its picks-and-shovels winners. GOOGL is primarily an AI application/hyperscaler and is not unambiguously implied as a direct infrastructure beneficiary by this specific thesis. Its inclusion appears to be bleed from adjacent themes.

META is an AI consumer/application company and a hyperscaler capex spender, but it is not an infrastructure beneficiary in the sense Baker's supercycle analog describes. Its inclusion is tangential to this specific infrastructure compounding thesis.

Similar to META and GOOGL, AMZN is a hyperscaler and AI consumer rather than a pure infrastructure beneficiary in the mold of Cisco, EMC, or Sun that Baker's analog highlights. Tangential to this specific thesis.
